data.title: UMH Properties: Q3 2025 Operating Update Confirms Sound Fundamentals
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2025, UMH Properties (NYSE:UMH)'s 6.375% fixed-rate Series D preferred shares (NYSE:UMH.PR.D) have performed in line with the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF), delivering a gain of about ~6%:

While the preferred shares have been publicly traded since 2018, the company is still active in issuing incremental Series D shares, raising $75,000 in Q3 2025 alone. With an extra 1% of Fed rate cuts priced in by Fed funds futures markets by the end of 2026, I think the current 7.04% yield on the preferred shares will become increasingly expensive from a funding perspective.

As such, I see the preferred shares trading close to par value once the Fed has finished its monetary policy normalization. Coupled with a solid preferred dividend coverage and sizable common equity cushion, I view the Series D shares as attractively valued, ranking them a Buy.

In this article I also discuss the appeal of the company's common stock after its poor recent performance so far in 2025. With normalized FFO on pace for further growth and an attractive enterprise-level valuation, I see the common stock's current valuation as attractive, ranking it a Buy.

Company Overview

You can access all UMH results on its website here. UMH Properties is a residential REIT focused on manufactured housing, primarily in Pennsylvania (30.2% of developed sites), Ohio (27.7%), and Indiana (15.5%):

The company's enterprise value amounts to $2.16 billion, of which 58% is funded with common equity, 15% with preferred equity, leaving just 27% for net debt.

Q3 2025 Operational Update

In its Q3 2025 operational update (which is not as detailed as regular earnings releases) UMH reported a 0.3% Q/Q decline in home rental occupancy to 94.1%, while same-property site occupancy edged up by 0.3% Q/Q to 88.5%, with the company highlighting:

We currently have approximately 130 homes on site and ready for occupancy and an additional 280 homes on site that are currently being installed. This inventory will allow us to continue our occupancy growth for the remainder of the year and into next year.

UMH further elaborated that occupancy gains and recent rent increases should result in a 10% Y/Y jump in October 2025 rental charges.

The company also raised $80.2 million through 5.85% bonds due in 2030, deploying $14.6 million in two Maryland manufactured home acquisitions.

Safety of preferred distributions

The company previously communicated a $0.96-1.04/share normalized FFO outlook for 2025, which already incorporated incremental common and preferred share issuance. As such, taking into account the stable operating performance in Q3 2025, it is reasonable to assume that UMH delivers normalized FFO of about $84 million this year.

Annual preferred dividends amount to roughly $20.5 million, indicating that normalized FFO before preferred distributions (~$104.5 million) provides a very healthy coverage ratio for preferred shareholders of 5.1x.

The coverage with common equity market capitalization is also robust, with UMH's market cap of ~$1.26 billion providing 3.9x coverage of the $322 million in preferred equity outstanding.

To conclude, UMH's preferred dividend arguably comes with above-average safety due to robust coverage metrics, low debt, and a focus on recession-resilient manufactured housing.

Series D preferred shares rating

As of the time of writing, the company's Series D preferred shares trade at $22.88/share, an 8.5% discount to par value, presenting a current yield of about 7.04%. This is roughly 1.2% above the last 5.85% debt issuance, or 2.4% above the company's 4.63% average cost of debt.

With market pricing reflecting a Fed funds rate of about 3% by late 2026, I believe the company's marginal cost of debt should decline to about 5%, likely making continued preferred share issuance at current prices quite expensive (in Q3 2025 the company issued preferred shares at a price of $23.00/share):

The current investment grade spread stands at 0.76%, while the spread of 30-year treasuries (US30Y) relative to ten year yields (US10Y) stands at 0.61%. I believe that combining the two spreads is a good approximation of the incremental spread a preferred security should trade at relative to debt. As such, assuming that UMH's marginal cost of debt declines to 5% in late 2026, a required rate of return of about 6.37% appears fair for the company's preferred shares.

As the Series D preferred shares offer a dividend of 6.375% at their par value of $25/share, I think it is reasonable to believe that the preferred shares trade close to par value in late 2026, providing a decent capital gain (roughly 9%) for investors buying at current levels.

What is more, current recession risks in the U.S. arguably remain above-average (driven by a weak labor market), which may result in the Fed moving to a below neutral rate. This may further increase the appeal of the fixed-rate preferred shares.

To conclude, I believe UMH's preferred shares offer a well-covered 7.04% current yield, with investors likely to realize some capital gains once the Fed is done with its policy normalization. As such, I believe a Buy rating is warranted for the Series D preferred shares.

The common shares

In contrast to the preferred shares, UMH's common stock has delivered a loss of 19% so far in 2025, significantly underperforming the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ):

Indeed 2025 has been challenging for residential REITs, with most REITs under my watch underperforming broad real estate benchmarks despite reasonable operating momentum. Case in point, at its guidance midpoint, UMH's normalized FFO is set to grow by 7.5% Y/Y in 2025. This makes the company's normalized FFO multiple of 14.7x quite attractive.

One downside of UMH is that it has very little floating rate debt, hence common shareholders will see no earnings boost from Fed rate cuts. Even so, the company reported same property net operating income [NOI] growth of 10% Y/Y in Q2 2025, benefitting from rising occupancy of its developed sites:

Obviously part of the NOI growth can be regarded as one-off since you can only increase occupancy up to a certain extent. Still I see the current normalized FFO multiple as attractive given the limited use of leverage and recession-resilience of manufactured housing.

Looking at the company's enterprise level cash flows, we observe that interest costs prior to the latest Q3 2025 issuance are running at about $30.5 million annually, which coupled with the $104.5 million of normalized FFO plus preferred dividend payments brings us to a cumulative enterprise-level cash flow figure of about $135 million.

Relative to the $2.16 billion enterprise value, we see that the market-implied cap rate stands at about 6.25% after administrative costs (which are already included in normalized FFO).

If we add back general and administrative costs of about $25 million annually, cumulative enterprise level cash flows increase to $160 million, representing a market-implied cap rate of 7.4% from an acquirer's perspective (a common approach when estimating net operating income).

In any case, while the current NOI growth rate will clearly not continue indefinitely, I view UMH's common stock as attractively valued both from a normalized FFO multiple and market-implied cap rate perspective, ranking it a Buy.

Risks

The preferred shares enjoy robust dividend coverage and a sizable common equity cushion. Coupled with solid operating momentum, I see little risks related to UMH's financial prospects. As such, the main risk in the investment case would be a slower-than-expected pace of Fed rate cuts. Indeed this would delay the potential for capital gains, while at the same time potentially exposing UMH to higher interest costs (which on the margin will reduce dividend coverage).

For the common shares the principal risk lies in continuing the strong operating performance observed in recent quarters. As hinted above, occupancy increases will eventually taper off, likely resulting in a meaningful reduction in NOI growth.

Last but not least, only 0.7% of debt is floating rate, implying that UMH will see no earnings boost from Fed rate cuts, in contrast to most small REIT peers.

Conclusion

UMH Properties' Series D preferred shares enjoy robust dividend coverage and a sizable common equity cushion. With roughly 1% in Fed rate cuts priced in by Fed funds futures markets by the end of 2026, the Series D preferreds offer an attractive current yield and some capital gain potential, warranting a Buy rating on my side.

The company's common stock has meaningfully underperformed broad real estate ETF benchmarks despite sound operating performance. This has resulted in the normalized FFO multiple dropping to just 14.7x, quite attractive in light of ongoing increases in net operating income. As such, I view recent underperformance as a Buying opportunity, putting a Buy rating on UMH common stock.

The pace of Fed rate cuts is a key risk factor to consider for the preferred shares, while maintaining sound operating performance will likely make or break the Buy case for the common shares.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Broadcom: AI Infrastructure Spend Set To Drive Significant Growth
data.description:
Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) is one of the best-positioned mega-cap tech companies on the market with a substantial growth opportunity in the coming years. With capital deployment across the hyperscalers expected to grow in the second half of 2025 and into 2026 to support their growing backlogs, Broadcom may realize growth acceleration for its AI semiconductors, particularly with the help of its recently announced $10 billion deal with Open AI. Given the strong growth momentum, I am upgrading my rating to a "Strong Buy" with a price target of $479/share at 33x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA. I last covered Broadcom in May with a "Buy" rating.

Broadcom Operations

Broadcom is deepening its exposure to the AI accelerator market beyond its hyperscaler customer base. The company announced plans to launch OpenAI’s first AI chip in 2026 for internal use, a potentially large and expansive market that can provide hyperscaler-like revenue growth. As part of Project Stargate, OpenAI is partnering with data center developer Oracle Corp. (ORCL) to build out its AI factories in the coming years. As denoted in Oracle’s q1’26 earnings release, the projects set for OpenAI have moved into backlog, sending the organization’s total backlog to $455 billion, well above any peer hyperscaler. Overall, the conversion into backlog tells me that the projects will be moving forward in the coming quarters or years and have been set in motion. Accordingly, the deal with OpenAI is set to bring in $10 billion in a one-time order. According to the Wall Street Journal, OpenAI has been working with Broadcom for over a year in developing these AI chips that are purpose-built for model training. Given that these chips will be used for internal purposes, I do not believe the partnership will cannibalize Nvidia’s (NVDA) presence with OpenAI, particularly following the $100 billion investment Nvidia announced in OpenAI.

Deepening its inter-data center connectivity capabilities, Broadcom began shipping the Jericho4 Ethernet fabric router in August 2025. The router was designed to support clusters beyond 200,000 compute nodes and distribute data across data centers, scaling to 36,000 HyperPorts while operating at 3.2Tb/s.

Given Broadcom’s strong trajectory for custom ASICs and enterprise software, the organization closed q3’25 with a $110 billion backlog. Considering AI semiconductor growth, I believe Broadcom should realize significant tailwinds in the coming quarters, particularly as each of the hyperscalers announced increased capital investments for e2h25 and 2026. Though Broadcom does not outright disclose its hyperscaler customers, we do know that Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft (MSFT), and Alphabet (GOOG) are guiding stronger capital deployments tied to compute capacity, suggesting that there may be stronger growth for Broadcom’s AI semiconductor business. Much of the growth capital is tied to the mounting backlogs across the hyperscalers, driving the need to scale their respective data center footprints to service customer demand.

At the end of August 2025, Broadcom introduced VMware Cloud Foundation [VCF] 9, a unified, integrated private cloud platform that can be deployed either in a private data center or in a cloud environment. The platform was designed to enable all application workloads, inclusive of AI workloads, in a secure environment. The platform provides enterprises with a private cloud alternative that can be utilized by sovereign customers. This can be a major benefit that can drive broad adoption across sovereign nations given their need for a private, secured cloud environment, potentially providing Broadcom a substantial opportunity for VCF 9 adoptions.

Broadcom Financial Position

Looking ahead, I’m forecasting Broadcom to generate $17.7 billion in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $1.88/share. My forecast assumes that margins may compress as a result of a higher product mix for AI XPU sales while non-AI semiconductor sales remain moderately flat. Looking further out, I’m forecasting significant growth in eFY26 driven by Broadcom’s recently announced deal with OpenAI for its custom AI chips. In addition to this, I’m forecasting strong growth across Broadcom’s 3 hyperscaler customers, particularly given the increased capital outlay for e2h25 and 2026.

Broadcom closed q3’25 with $10.7 billion in cash & equivalent and $64 billion in debt on the balance sheet for a leverage ratio of 1.34x net debt/aEBITDA, a significant improvement when compared to the previous year’s quarter. Broadcom experienced a modest working capital build in q3’25, primarily the result of inventory builds for deliveries expected in eq4’25. Given the $10 billion deal with OpenAI, I’m forecasting inventory to build going into the end of the fiscal year as the firm ramps up production to service this large contract.

Risks Related to Broadcom

Bull Case

Broadcom’s addressable market is expanding as hyperscalers increase capital deployment for compute capacity to service their growing backlogs, driving significant top-line growth in the coming years. Broadcom also gained a major $10 billion contract with OpenAI for custom AI semiconductors that may result in additional growth as OpenAI continues to expand its AI capabilities. With AI development competition becoming more fierce, Broadcom may be positioned to take on additional LLM customers in the coming quarters or years if capacity constraints remain a hindrance.

Bear Case

The deal with OpenAI may turn out to be a one-time deal that may not transition into periods beyond the $10 billion contract. Adoption of custom ASICs may slow if Nvidia’s latest AI accelerator iterations outpace Broadcom’s technology capabilities.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

AVGO shares are currently trading at the upper end of their historical trading range at 42.46x EV/aEBITDA, making shares relatively expensive when compared to historical averages.

Considering peer comps, AVGO shares are trading at a substantial premium to the peer average of 35x EV/EBITDA on an unadjusted basis. Considering its two larger peers, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Nvidia (NVDA), AVGO shares remain relatively expensive.

Despite the premium valuation, I believe AVGO shares may have some room for growth given the growth trajectory laid out. In the mid-term, shares may gyrate at their current level or realize some pullback given the significant trading premium, creating an opportunity for investors to average into a position. Given the strong outlook, I am recommending AVGO shares with a Strong Buy with a price target of $479/share at 33x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative marketcap and relative multiple.

data.title: Blackstone Secured Lending: Buy The Dip On This 12% Yield
data.description:
I’ve largely stayed on the sidelines on BDCs over the past several months, as risks from lower interest rates combined with high valuations had given me pause. However, thanks to the market pendulum that consistently swings from one end to another, many BDC valuations have become compelling again. This has resulted in buying opportunities as of late, especially as yields on many have reached the low teens.

This brings me to Blackstone Secured Lending (NYSE:BXSL), which I last covered back in November 2024, highlighting its portfolio quality, NAV per share growth, and conservative balance sheet. The last couple of months haven’t been kind to the stock, as it now sits just shy of its 52-week low at the current price of $26.00. As shown below, this has pushed BXSL’s dividend yield up to 11.8%.

In this article, I revisit BXSL including recent business results, and discuss what makes it an excellent high yielding bargain after the recent dip in price, so let’s get started!

Why BXSL?

Blackstone Secured Lending is a BDC that’s externally managed by Blackstone (BX). It provides direct financing solutions to privately-held U.S. middle market companies. BXSL stands out for having very high exposure to the safer first lien secured debt, which represents 98.2% of the total portfolio. This sits significantly higher than the 76% and 59% first-lien exposure of large peers Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC) and Ares Capital (ARCC).

At present, BXSL has a $13.3 billion investment fair value that’s spread across 295 portfolio companies. The portfolio maintains a safe weighted average loan-to-value ratio of 46.9%, giving BXSL plenty of equity buffer to protect against losses. No single investment represents over 3% of the portfolio and the portfolio is well-diversified across a mix of growth and defensive industries. As shown below, top industries include software, healthcare, professional services, insurance, and commercial services.

Despite the impact of lower interest rates, BXSL still managed to produce an annualized ROE of 11.2% during Q2 2025 (down from 13.2% in the prior year period). NII per share came in at $0.77, which fully covered the $0.77 quarterly dividend rate at a 100% payout ratio.

Admittedly, the payout ratio has become tighter compared to the past 3 years, when rising rates provided a boost to NII per share, as shown below. However, it’s worth noting that BXSL has $1.86 per share of spillover income. This equates to 2.5 quarters of dividends in a hypothetical situation should NII go to zero.

Nonetheless, BXSL is finding no shortage of deals, as portfolio value grew by 17% YoY to $13.3 billion. Net funded investment activity was $345 million. This was supported by 15 new portfolio borrowers, which more than offset 4 exits.

True to its form, nearly all new investments were first lien secured loans with average loan-to-value below 40%, reinforcing BXSL’s conservative stance. Notably, Q2’s largest deal was a $500 million financing for Acuity Delivery Systems. The deal showcases BXSL’s ability to provide one-stop, flexible financing, backed by deep healthcare expertise across Blackstone’s credit platform.

At the same time, portfolio credit remains sound, with investments on non-accrual representing just 0.3% and 0.1% of portfolio cost and fair value, respectively. Importantly, 93% of investment income came from steady cash-paying interest rather than PIK (payment in kind) or fees, which are lumpy in nature. NAV per share declined slightly by $0.06 sequentially, but is up by 1% YoY. As shown below, BXSL has maintained overall strong NAV per share growth/preservation since inception.

Looking ahead, BXSL is well-positioned to grow the portfolio. This is supported by sharply rising pipeline activity, with deal screenings up 50% from late 2024. This signals stronger deployment ahead. This is supported by BXSL’s access to Blackstone’s Credit and Investment arm, as noted during the last earnings call:

In an increasingly competitive private credit market, we believe we differentiate ourselves as not just a lender but as a value-added partner, helping credits grow equity value. As we have mentioned in the past, all BXCI private credit investments, including BXSL portfolio companies have full access to the value creation program at no additional cost to the borrower. We offer this because we understand the significant value this program can bring to the investment portfolio.

Importantly, BXSL carries a BBB- with positive outlook credit rating from S&P and a Baa2 rating from Moody’s. This is supported by a safe debt-to-equity ratio of 1.13x, which sits in the middle of management’s 1.0x to 1.25x target range. It also sits significantly below the 2.0x statutory limit for BDCs. Plus, BXSL has $3 billion in total liquidity including cash and undrawn credit capacity.

Lastly, I see value in BXSL after the recent drop in price to $26.00, equating to a price-to-book value of 0.95x. As shown below, this sits at the low end of BXSL’s 5-year trading range.

At the current price, the market appears to have baked in plenty of pessimism around the stock, pushing the dividend yield up to 11.8%. Risks to the thesis include lower interest rates as well as a shakier job market. This could result in economic headwinds that could impact the financial health of some borrowers. Moreover, increased competition for deals could be a headwind for portfolio yields on debt investment.

However, I see these headwinds as having been baked into the stock price. Plus, BXSL has a strong credit portfolio that’s comprised primarily of first-lien secured debt. This helps to buffer against potential losses. Moreover, it has plenty of spillover income, as noted earlier, to protect the dividend. While lower rates are a headwind, this could be partially offset by lower cost of BXSL’s own debt. This could help BXSL to maintain a healthy investment spread.

Investor Takeaway

Blackstone Secured Lending is currently a compelling high-yield opportunity after its recent price drop to near 52-week lows, pushing its dividend yield to an attractive 11.8%. BXSL maintains a conservative, high-quality portfolio with 98% first-lien secured loans, low non-accruals, and solid diversification. Its strong underwriting, ample liquidity, and spillover income offer a cushion for the dividend and downside protection. Trading at just 0.95x book value and with a stable NAV, BXSL offers investors a solid chance to buy a top-tier BDC at a discount. As such, I rate BXSL as a ‘Buy’ for high yield and value.

data.title: The ADP Report Says Layoffs Are Starting, Recession Is Near
data.description:
The layoffs are starting

The ADP Report showed that private employers shed -32K jobs in September, for the second consecutive month of job losses, which is the first time since the 2020 recession.

That's what happens in recessions; people start losing their jobs. Why do people start losing their jobs? The businesses experience lower activity and reduce their headcount. For example, a restaurant with 10 permanent staff members sees lower traffic and thus reduces the headcount to 9. The person fired now does not have a paycheck and consumes much less, which triggers a vicious cycle of rising unemployment throughout the economy.

There have been so-called technical recessions where growth is negative, but employment stays solid, and that's not worrisome. But growth cannot remain solid when jobs are lost. And that's the current situation: growth is still solid, but the layoffs are just starting, according to ADP, so a recession is increasingly likely.

Can the ADP report be trusted?

By the way, the US government is in a shutdown, so we will not get the September labor market from the BLS on Friday, and most likely we will not get the October labor market as well.

Thus, we need to rely on private data, and ADP is the main labor market data provider. But can we trust the ADP report?

I never really paid much attention to the ADP report, mostly because the financial media would always caution that the ADP report is not really correlated with the official NFP BLS report.

The ADP data is available on the FRED database from February 2010 to the present, so I pulled the ADP data and the official NFP data and computed the correlation between the two variables, and the correlation is 0.75. That is a very high correlation, meaning the two series tend to move together most of the time.

I also computed the average monthly change in jobs for each index, and ADP shows 148K on average added since 2/2010, while NFP shows 159K jobs on average. When considering that NFP includes government jobs, and ADP doesn't, that's nearly an identical number. The correlation would likely increase towards 1 if you exclude government jobs for NFP.

The point is ADP data can be trusted; it's nearly identical to the NFP data.

Here is the visual over the last 5 years: ADP in a blue solid line and NFP in a dashed line—they seem highly correlated.

How is the ADP report computed?

This is what ADP self-reports:

The ADP National Employment Report (NER) presents independent measures of the U.S. labor market based on ADP payroll data covering more than half a million companies with more than 26 million employees.

The ADP NER provides a high-frequency, weekly measure of U.S. private-sector employment. In addition, it presents regular measures of wages or earnings for defined samples or segments of the U.S. workforce.

ADP payroll data includes payroll transactions data – when a person is paid and how much – as well as administrative data on who is on the company payroll (even if they are not paid in the current pay period), and characteristics of the employer and employee.

So, ADP reports the actual payroll data for half a million companies with 26 million employees, and it's a high-frequency weekly measure.

The BLS labor market report is based on two surveys, where the response rate has been declining.

Thus, it appears that the ADP report is more reliable, but the fact that the two reports are highly correlated is a vote of confidence for the survey-based BLS report, which has been criticized lately due to major revisions.

What did the ADP report find?

The ADP report is actually very informative and thus useful.

Based on the September ADP report, most job losses are concentrated in small companies. Large companies still continue to add jobs.

This finding confirms the thesis that the current macro environment favors the largest companies, while the small companies struggle. In fact, the tax law in the BBB favors the largest companies with heavy Capex. In addition, tariffs tend to hurt the small companies the most.

However, this situation is unsustainable, as job losses from small and mid-size companies are likely to affect the business of the large companies as the unemployment rate increases.

In addition, the ADP report finds that cyclical goods-producing industries like construction and manufacturing lost jobs in September; this is consistent with an unfolding recession.

ADP also finds that most service jobs were created in healthcare, and this is mostly due to the structural event, aging demographics. All other service industries lost jobs, excluding a small gain in information. Thus, the ADP report paints a grim picture of private job creation, consistent with a recession.

Reaction from the financial markets

The interest rates fell after the ADP report. United States 10-Year Bond Yield (US10Y) fell to 4.11%, driven by a decrease in real rates, and United States 2-Year Bond Yield (US2Y) fell to 3.54% anticipating deeper Fed cuts. This reaction is consistent with a risk-off, safe-haven trade anticipating a recession.

Gold (GLD) closed at a new record high, and this reflects an ultimate safe-haven trade.

The S&P 500 (SP500) closed at a new time high, completely ignoring the ADP report, shutdown, or any other macro risk. However, under the hood, the positive performance was led by the Healthcare sector (XLV) in reaction to the positive news on tariffs. The Tech sector (XLK) was led higher by the semiconductors, while the Discretionary sector (XLY) was led by Tesla, Inc. (TSLA).

All other sectors finished lower for the day (8 out of 11 lower). So, that's the market, highly concentrated and speculative.

Implications

The ADP report is signaling a recession, and this is consistent with other data. The economy is still growing, but that's likely to change, as the weakening labor market ultimately causes a recession, based on the historical evidence.

Thus, investors should brace for recession and the recessionary bear market, as the current rally is clearly unsustainable—the Shiller P/E ratio is now above 40 for the second time in history, behind the 2000 dot-com bubble.

data.title: Getty Realty: Upgrading This High-Quality REIT To A Strong Buy On Macro Improvements, Great Yield
data.description:
Introduction

Since I first covered them early in July and called Getty Realty (NYSE:GTY) an “Undervalued REIT Poised For A Recovery,” the stock is down a bit despite positive macro developments and overall mixed results.

Still, the opportunity remains, and with a slightly better dividend yield now given the current price combined with significant developments to one of their key near-term catalysts, I believe it’s worth taking another look into GTY to update the thesis.

Recent Quarter

Their AFFO increased to $33.97 million in Q2 2025 (~$0.59 per share) and they even increased the lower end of the AFFO guidance to a range of $2.40 to $2.41 per diluted share from the previous $2.38 to $2.41 - so just getting more clarity now and narrowing it to a very clear point. For 55.6 million diluted shares outstanding as of their Q2 report, that’s an AFFO of about $133.72 at midpoint, which is not bad at all for a market cap of $1.52 billion.

That also means that the company will deliver a decent ~2.77% increase in AFFO per share compared to last year, which means that the dividend still remains very sustainable. For a now 7% yield, that’s overall a very solid payout ratio (~77.77%) and they can even increase it a bit while still remaining in a very comfortable range, while the rest can be used to repay debt or take advantage of potential opportunities.

I say that as the company didn’t really deploy that much capital YTD, although the ~8.1% cash yield they did it at is not bad at all. This comes as the company issued more stock this year compared to previous ones, with ~$240 million in capital deployments (equity and debt) committed earlier this year to fund its investment pipeline.

Their balance sheet also didn’t really change since the initial analysis, with an overall solid balance sheet although they have quite some leverage, albeit we have to keep in mind that this is due from 2028 onward and there’s plenty of time for them to repay or refinance some of it, as needed. From the looks of it, they are relatively in line with their leverage targets, as they say in the Q2 report:

Moving to the balance sheet and liquidity. At quarter end, net debt to EBITDA was 5.2x or 4.6x, taking into account unsettled forward equity. We continue to target leverage of 4.5 to 5.5x net debt to EBITDA and are well positioned to maintain those levels going forward.

Still, the company’s overall quality didn’t really change (and it’s nearly impossible for it to change quarter over quarter), their occupancy rate is still about 99.7%, and I’d argue that the recent macro developments were overall a positive that the market doesn’t seem to price in, so there’s potential to see some “pent-up” demand for their stock that could eventually manifest when the right catalyst triggers.

Macro Developments

The main near-term catalyst that I cited in the previous analysis are rate cuts. Following the September cut, the Fed now expects 2 more cuts this year despite Powell seeing “meaningful” inflation ahead due especially to tariffs. Rate cuts are very important for a company like Getty Realty, as the demand for their properties and tenants’ services are both affected by a high-rate environment. A recovery in that combined with directly reducing the interest on their debt and the debt of their tenants (which really need it, as some even filed for bankruptcy in recent years), while allowing them to explore getting more leverage if needed to expand.

Plus, this also advanced another closely related catalyst: lower treasury yields. As rates are cut, treasury yields will also fall, meaning that the theoretical risk-free rate will drop, resulting in the market having to adjust their valuations for the new risk-adjusted results since the risk-free rate drops down. This can result in more demand especially for dividend stocks and REITs in particular, as their relative safety, low leverage and high yields could be seen as "next in line" after treasuries when it comes to risk-adjusted yields.

Still, I’d like to emphasize that the company still has fixed-rate debt maturities coming only from 2028 onward, so they have time to wait for more rate cuts before worrying about refinancing. And when these positive changes start to result in more activity, the company even said that they’re ready to be there for their tenants as they get back to expansion:

When you look at the performance of our tenants, right, there continue to be healthy businesses operating in sectors that have a lot of positive fundamentals and even overall macro themes, right, that they're continuing to benefit from. And as these folks look to either grow through M&A or through new store builds, we've been a very consistent capital provider in our target markets. And I think what we're seeing now is folks are kind of returning towards growth and some of our consistent presence and dialogue with repeat business and existing tenants and even new tenants, you're starting to see that flow through various stages in our pipeline.

The fact that they didn’t really react to rate cuts can also be seen as a positive, although we have to think about why that was the case. Given the nature of the business, GTY is very deeply tied to the economy, and although rate cuts are theoretically good for that, what can come before the beneficial effects of rate cuts remains uncertain. The market is very scared right now (see gold breaching record after record and reaching ~$3,900 a few days ago) due to risk of a hard landing, which can lead to bad (even worse) environments for the company, such as a recession. This comes on top of the pressure coming from Trump’s tariffs potentially leading to inflation, and a few days ago we’ve also seen another headwind arise.

That is the US Congress failing to pass either a full appropriations bill or a stopgap measure (Continuing Resolution) before the start of the fiscal year. That wouldn’t be Trump’s only government shutdown, but it comes with even more political uncertainty in a time when the market is probably full of it. In short, the federal government shut down for the first time since 2019 after a congressional deadlock over funding, triggered by the Democrats' demands to extend Affordable Care Act subsidies and reverse Medicaid cuts, and is compounded by an administration threat of permanent layoffs for federal workers.

To be honest, what it does is just further weaken the US’ image internationally, with a dollar that’s already weak and gold reaching over $3,900 recently. I don’t think it’s very likely, but it (combined with other factors) could eventually degenerate into much less trust in the country, which leads to money moving abroad, looking for better risk-adjusted investments.

Reiterated Valuation

Since nothing really changed other than a few current numbers, I’ll refresh the previous valuation. I started with a $133.72 million AFFO in 2025 - in line with their guidance - projecting 5.5% CAGR for the first 5 years (rate cuts, improving economy, growing with their tenants), followed by 4% for the subsequent 5, and applying a 3% terminal growth rate, with a 7.5% WACC (assumptions: ~9.2% cost of equity, ~4.8% cost of debt), plus $928.6 in net debt and 56.6 million shares outstanding. This results in an Enterprise Value of $3.55 billion. After subtracting the net debt, the Equity Value is $2.62 billion, leading to $46.23 per share, which is an excellent return compared to the current level.

Conclusions

Overall, Getty Realty remains a very solid investment for me, and I will actually upgrade it to a Strong Buy thanks to the macro improvements that came earlier than anticipated, while the stock barely moved. Their financials are good, the properties are nearly fully occupied, the tenants are fine, and the macro tailwinds can offer significant potential for improvements, and I’ll soon open a position.

data.title: Brown-Forman: Long-Term Initiatives Are Promising, But Near-Term Weakness Can't Be Ignored (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Brown-Forman Corporation (NYSE:BF.B) was a sell rating because I saw visible weakness in the business—pricing and volume pressure, which was made worse with the soft high-margin used barrel sales. So far, management has launched a series of strategic initiatives aimed at stabilizing and reaccelerating growth. These include revitalizing Jack Daniel’s with Tennessee Blackberry, a broader brand refresh, and a US go-to-market overhaul. The long-term plan is credible, but the near-term operating backdrop remains challenged, and visibility for a recovery is still low. As such, I am only upgrading to hold.

Strategic Initiatives Show Path to Recovery

The good news is that BF.B is taking meaningful steps to drive future growth inflection, in particular, efforts to reinvigorate its flagship Jack Daniel’s brand. The launch of Jack Daniel’s Tennessee Blackberry is proof that efforts are translating into results. This is the brand’s first major flavor innovation in over five years, and early traction has been encouraging, with management describing the start as incredibly promising and shipments already surpassing expectations. My view on this Blackberry launch is positive, as the blackberry flavor is one that consumers are gravitating towards, even in the alcoholic space. Even large beverage players like Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) mentioned that consumer interest in blackberry-flavored products is growing faster. Also, one additional note is that the flavor mixes well with lemonade, which I expect to increase the number of repeatable consumption occasions beyond just shots (it's tough to drink it neat all the time).

Riding on this new product launch, BF.B also did a brand refresh for Jack Daniels. And encouragingly, the new global campaign is already showing solid traction. Of which, what I find most compelling is how balanced the response has been. Younger legal-drinking-age consumers (up to 34) are clearly engaging with the message, and at the same time, scores among the core 35+ base are improving as well. In other words, the brand is finding relevance with the next generation without risking the loyalty of its longtime drinkers. This is significantly important because there has been the bearish narrative that younger generation consumers are drinking less. Hence, this is a solid development. Also, the fact that BF.B managed to not alienate those who have been loyal to Jack really shows that this new marketing campaign is working very well.

At the same time, BF.B is reshaping its go-to-market [GTM] model in the US for the first time in sixty years. Under the new structure, management expects an ~3x increase in headcount dedicated to its portfolio, coupled with a significantly simplified sales book in key markets. Now, we do not know the effectiveness of this yet, but I could see how these would help rejuvenate growth. The practical benefits are twofold. First, it gives Tennessee Blackberry the launch support to build sustained velocity rather than just trial. Second, it creates the runway for smaller, super-premium labels such as Gin Mare and Diplomático to finally scale with consistent attention. While the transition (effective August 1, 2025) may cause short-term disruption, the timeline for normalization is by the end of 2Q26. If that timeline holds, BF.B should be able to start delivering positive growth.

Weaknesses

While management’s long-term actions are positive, the near-term reality is still tough. Some bulls may argue that Q1 FY26 performance was alright—organic sales growth [OSG] saw 1% y/y—but I don’t think that is true. The 1% OSG was supported by a sizeable 4-point benefit from distributor inventory build, which is obviously not sustainable. Stripping that out, like-for-like OSG was down 3% y/y. Even more concerning is that both the US and developed international segments saw underlying sales fall 8% y/y, with major markets like Germany down 13% and the UK down 16% as consumer demand remains weak. Third-party data also backs this up, showing spirits consumption and brand volumes still running negative y/y through September. In short, the demand backdrop I noted previously has not turned the corner.

The used barrel business, which I previously highlighted as a risk, has now become a clear earnings headwind. Sales dropped more than 40% y/y in Q1, and management reiterated guidance for the full year to be down by “more than half.” This alone represents a meaningful profit drag that will be tough for BFB to close that gap. Which means, whatever traction they see with the new Jack Daniel's brand relaunch, the growth has to be monumental to drive growth at the consolidated level.

If these are not bad enough, consider that the trade dispute with Canada continues to shut American spirits off shelves, and BF.B is clearly hurt by that since sales there collapsed ~60% y/y. These issues, taken together, create a tough operating backdrop. BF.B may be executing well for a healthier long-term growth trajectory, but the reality is that pricing pressure, unfavorable mix, collapsing ancillary profit streams (used barrel sales), and macro-driven demand softness are immediate and tangible headwinds. Until there is clearer evidence of stabilization in developed markets, visibility remains poor, and I expect investor sentiment to stay capped.

Valuation

The way to think about how much negatives the market has already priced into BF.B shares can be seen when compared to peers (Diageo Plc and Pernod Ricard). Clearly, as you can see from the chart above, BF.B went from trading within a range of 17-33% premium, to just 10% today. And I believe this is because BF.B is the only player among the three to have low-single-digits EBITDA growth outlook for the next 2 years, vs. Diageo at high-single-digits CAGR and Pernod Ricard at mid-single-digits CAGR. At 12.5x forward EBITDA, with reduced expectations, I think it is hard to continue re-echoing a sell rating. Furthermore, I have to acknowledge that management is doing a seemingly good job in rejuvenating growth. The near term is without a doubt weak, but I think the market has already priced in this weakness, and as such, I am upgrading to hold. I will be monitoring how the long-term initiatives play out in the P&L, and once we see signs of things turning around (because of those initiatives), BF.B could become an interesting long.

Conclusion

Overall, I am moving my stance on BF.B to a hold. Management is executing on thoughtful initiatives that should, over time, reestablish brand momentum and open new growth avenues. However, these efforts are playing out against an environment of falling developed-market demand, collapsing used barrel sales, and ongoing trade friction in Canada. Overall, I believe the stock reflects much of this weakness. For now, I see little downside but also limited upside until proof of turnaround emerges.

data.title: U-Haul Holding Company Is Severely Undervalued (Upgrade)
data.description:
I don't know about you, but I always appreciate companies that have interesting business models. One such example can be seen by looking at U-Haul Holding Company (NYSE:UHAL) (NYSE:UHAL.B), a firm that operates primarily as a do-it-yourself moving business that also has a self-storage division. In recent years, management has achieved consistent growth on the top line. But bottom-line results have admittedly been a bit mixed. There is evidence that there is weakness in its markets. But at the end of the day, shares are incredibly high on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms.

In the past, I have been bullish on the company. In fact, as recently as May of this year, I called it a ‘buy’ candidate because of its underlying fundamentals and valuation. Since then, however, the stock has fallen by 13.6% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 14.3%. Because of this drop and because of a change in the assessment of how other players in the industries that it plays in look from a valuation standpoint, I actually think it would be appropriate to upgrade the company at this time to a ‘strong buy.’ This is especially true when you consider that the enterprise has seen its share price dropped 25.6% over the last year alone.

A Good Business At A Great Price

Fundamentally speaking, the data for U-Haul has been admittedly mixed as of late. Consider, for instance, revenue. During the most recent quarter, which would be the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, the company generated revenue of $1.63 billion. That's 5.3% above the $1.55 billion generated the previous year. This was driven by growth across all major categories. Management actually breaks out revenue, as you can tell by the image below, into multiple categories. But I would just focus on the core operations of the business, which would be those involving its self-moving equipment rental business and its self-storage business.

For the most recent quarter, self-moving equipment rental revenue came in at $1.06 billion. This represents an increase of 4.3% compared to the $1.01 billion generated the previous year. According to management, this was thanks to multiple contributors. For instance, the firm benefited from a rise in revenue per transaction for both in-town and one-way markets. In addition to this, they also saw a benefit from an increase in the number of company-operated retail locations, independent dealers, and more.

When it comes to the self-storage side of the business, we do have more data. According to management, overall sales came in at $234.2 million. That's 8.6% above the $215.7 million the company reported the previous year. During this time, the company saw a benefit from a rise in the number of units that it has in operation from 748 to 813, taking the total square footage that the business has from 63.6 million to 69.6 million. We did see an unfortunate decline in the occupancy rate, with the average monthly occupancy rate per unit dropping from 80% to 78.1%. But the rise in the number of units more than offset this to push revenue higher.

One thing that does muddy the waters in terms of my analysis is the fact that management lumps in certain products and services that it makes available for customers into one category that includes both self-moving and self-storage. This is frustrating because it gives us an incomplete picture and makes comparative analysis using a sum-of-the-more-parts approach more difficult. For the purpose of this analysis, anything that cannot be tied solely to the self-storage side of the business will be assumed to fall under the self-moving side. This will probably give us a more conservative assessment than if we had more granular data from management. I would rather be overly conservative in my assessment and wrong in a positive way than be overly liberal in my assessment and wrong in a negative way.

The most exciting part of this would be its U-Box program, which helped grow other revenue from $133.2 million to $154.1 million. This is sort of a hybrid business, offering customers portable storage and moving containers. In the most recent quarter alone, sales for it were up about $21 million, or 16% year over year. This means that substantially all of the revenue under the ‘other’ category is associated with U-Box. Seeing as how that is at least a core part of the business, and given my view that it more aligns with the self-moving equipment side of things as opposed to the self-storage side, I will include it in my valuation.

Even though revenue for the company improved year over year, net profits did contract from $195.4 million to $142.3 million. This drop in profitability is disappointing, to say the least. According to the data provided, the firm saw major increases in key categories. Primarily, interest expense rose from $67.2 million to $82.3 million because of an increase in overall debt. In addition to this, the company saw a rise in operating expenses from $789.8 million to $826.7 million. And it saw depreciation grow from $216.5 million to $304 million. Other profitability metrics were better. Operating cash flow grew from $453.9 million to $598.4 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $468.9 million to $485.8 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $514.7 million to $545.3 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2024 and 2025 fiscal years. Revenue increased, but profitability declined. The good news is that some of the cash flow metrics, but not all of them, improved on a year-over-year basis. You can also see, in the images below, some of the contributors to the increase in financial performance from one year to the next. I could dig into this more, but I think a better use of our time would be to look at other data instead.

Primarily, I'm interested in how the company looks from a valuation standpoint. In the chart below, you can see how the firm is valued on the whole. This looks at the picture through the lens of the 2025 fiscal year and what I estimate for the 2026 fiscal year. But before I get to that, I do think a little bit of detail about these markets is appropriate. To be honest with you, detail on the self-moving side is a bit difficult to come by. But what I do know is that, in theory, the consumer self-moving industry that U-Haul caters to relies heavily on broader economic activity.

Specifically, I'm referring to things like residential activity. And given the state of the economy, that has been an area of weakness. Although we recently saw a surge in new home sales in August of this year compared to August of last year, with overall sales up 15.4% on a year-over-year basis, on a year-over-year basis new home sales are down 1.4% for the year-to-date period. Housing completions are down 8.4% year over year for the first eight months of the year. Housing starts are down 6%, while building permits are down a whopping 11.1%. And according to the National Association of Realtors, for four of the first eight months of this year, existing home sales have declined. And another month, they were flat. None of this bodes well for this market.

The same kind of issue exists with the self-storage space. But we do have a lot more data here, thanks to a competitor by the name of Extra Space Storage (EXR). It currently operates as the largest player in the market with a 15.3% stake. By comparison, U-Haul is the 4th largest, with a share of 4.3%.

Although occupancy rates seemed to be weakening, which conforms with what U-Haul has demonstrated, the long-term outlook here is at least positive. Back in 1987, only about 2.7% of all US households used storage services. But by 2023, that number had increased to 12.6%. Long term, I expect this trend to continue. And that should definitely benefit firms such as U-Haul.

When it comes to valuing the company, these two different business models make it a bit complex. We know that the firm is valued at low multiples on an absolute basis. But to understand just how much upside potential might exist, we should break it up into its two different end markets. In the first chart below, you can see EV to EBITDA multiples for the three largest players in the self-storage space. And in the chart below that, you can see the same thing for the three firms that I think are most comparable to U-Haul from a self-moving perspective.

As these charts illustrate, there are significant valuation differences between these two types of businesses. And it makes sense. The fact of the matter is that the self-storage space is pretty low in terms of capital intensity. It is a pretty stable market, and cash flows are significant. At the same time, the self-moving space is more capital-intensive and offers lower margins. It's also more susceptible to volatile swings in the economy.

With this in mind, we can see in the chart above how much U-Haul’s portion of these respective businesses would be worth if the firm were to trade at the lowest of the multiples of these companies and if it were to trade at the average of them. For context, the company currently has an enterprise value of only $16.57 billion. In the chart below, you can see how much upside we would get if the firm were to be broken up into two separate businesses or if it were to be valued according to how the market is valuing these other players. This is a massive amount of upside if you ask me.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I would argue that U-Haul is a solid business with excellent upside potential. The market does not seem to be valuing the firm appropriately. And because of this, I have no choice but to rate it a ‘strong buy’ as opposed to the ‘buy’ rating I had on it previously. I have not made the decision to buy into it yet. But I am certainly thinking it over.

data.title: The Mirage Of Income Agnosticism: What MARO Vs. NVDY Really Reveal
data.description:
I am attempting an odd comparison between a pair of YieldMax single stock based option ETFs in this thesis. Odd, because MARA and Nvidia have little in common fundamentally, but the YieldMax MARA Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:MARO) and the YieldMax NVDA Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:NVDY) do. Both the option ETFs have an income centric objective. I intend to check how far the underlying impacts the income strategy. Ideally, income strategies should be underlying agnostic as much as possible. The strategy, theoretically, should also not require a bull thesis in the underlying. But how does that translate in real market conditions?

Another comparison the choice of Nvidia versus MARA enables, is how volatility impacts total returns and hence performance of the option strategies. It is expected that higher volatility is good for such plans because there is more premium to be earned. But does that really translate to expected gains when the rubber hits the road?

I intend to answer these common perceptions in this thesis and hope the pointers and conclusion help set expectations from single stock option income strategies across the board. MARO is of course based on a high volatility underlying (somewhere around MSTY levels) and NVDY is based on a sufficiently volatile underlying (i.e. not too benign, yet not as volatile as MARO of MSTY). The comparison between them should help study two typical moderate to high volatility popular stock based strategies.

Volatility and Requirement of a Bull Thesis

Below is a 30 day rolling volatility chart comparing MARA and Nvidia. I have included MSTR in there as well to show how high MARA's volatility is - almost at par and even higher for most part of the last one year. The correlated volatility patterns between the two is derived from the bitcoin connection. The magnitude of MARA's volatility is not by any means lower than MSTR's leveraged bitcoin exposure- so MARA does serve as a proxy for an underlying with a "very high" volatility profile.

Nvidia, also a volatile stock on its own, dwarfs in volatility terms, when compared to MARA. The consolidation in the past few months have meant even further reduction in Nvidia's volatility, which even otherwise often trades at half the volatility levels MARA does. The two stocks chosen for our study are clearly examples of a very high and a reasonably high underlying. That should put to test how high volatility impacts income performance.

To compare how MARO and NVDY perform over various market conditions, I have charted total returns for both since mid February 2024. That captures both a correction phase till April 2025 and a subsequent rebound, both of reasonable intensity. And the differences tell a story. MARO hardly delivers any alpha in drawdowns. In the same period NVDY does deliver a reasonable 4 percentage point cushion over Nvidia.

On the way up, MARO traces MARA slightly better than NVDY traces Nvidia, give or take. I am talking about average consistent upside capture, not the current end points alone. MARO may look to be hugging total returns for MARA more closely, but that may have to do with the smaller rebound of ~60% in MARA compared to ~90% in Nvidia (also growing almost monotonically without break).

To compare upside capture better, I have broken down the rebound from April 2025 up to July 2025, to compare similar upsides in the underlying and remove any performance differences due to to differences in the return trajectories of the underlying themselves. For a similar upside in Nvidia and MARA, MARO's ability to capture upside better is visibly obvious through this phase. So much so that, barring a few periods, MARO almost completely traces MARA.

Let us understand what is going on behind the scenes. MARA's higher volatility means higher premiums, which explains the higher upside capture compared to NVDY. The same higher premium is expected to help reduce drawdowns on the downside, but does not - this is because in a high volatility underlying, even a correcting trajectory is often a juxtaposition of volatile up and down moves, making premium capture difficult compared to a monotonic decrease on share prices. This shows that in real market conditions, higher volatility alone is not enough to sustain higher income or performance of the cash covered call position.

Conclusion 1: Option Strategies rely more on appreciation of the underlying than the option premium. The option premium that you expect in flat to correcting markets is often missing - as seen in the case of MARO. Read here on how the option income often goes missing in flat markets as well.

Conclusion 2: Since the YieldMax strategies (or any call writing strategy) relies on the appreciation of the underlying, it requires a bull thesis in the underlying in a vast majority of cases. A bearish underlying definitely does not work. A moderately bullish or neutral underlying may work well even in cases of consolidation like NVDY does.

Conclusion 3: A higher volatility underlying like MARA often ends up gaining almost fully from the total returns of the underlying - the option gains are difficult to lock in over time because of the underlying's volatile moves.

Summarizing, higher volatility may work if the underlying thesis is bullish. Lower volatility underlying stands a chance for agnostic income, but still required a neutral to mildly bullish thesis on the underlying. This generalization is not the only factor to ascertain performance expectations - it is a broad guideline. There are other executional aspects and strategy nuances that impact performance. Eventually, in real market conditions, each unique trajectory of the same underlying often yields different performances in different regimes. But broadly expect lower volatility underlying (or let us say optimal volatility underlying - not too high) stands a better chance of performing irrespective of a Bull thesis. That is NVDY may work, GOOY may not (too low volatility = too low premiums to cushion downsides or capture upsides) and so won't MARO (too high) if not for a bullish thesis on MARA.

How to Use MARO and NVDY

MARO suffers on drawdowns due to too high volatility levels in MARA. It does capture upsides better (though not flawless really). But all in all, it eventually ends up doing a MARA at best. The ~36% yield on cost basis for a 1 year horizon is largely funded by capital appreciation in MARA than the option layer. MARO has nothing better to offer over MARA overall in the best case, where it becomes almost redundant eventually. Hence I rate it a Sell - suffering from similar issues that a higher volatility underlying in the case of MSTY leads to.

It is not like NVDY is an outright Buy on the other hand. I maintain a Buy rating on it, even after a reduction in volatility levels in the past few months, after my last thesis. NVDY also needs a somewhat bullish to neutral thesis in Nvidia and is a Buy for a tactical use case - i.e., those who are fearful of heights yet bullish on Nvidia or find the stock overheated and consolidating for the next few quarters. NVDY may do well for a slow consolidation or slow correction in Nvidia wherein it can gain a few percentage points from the option layer. But even NDVY cannot do much if Nvidia falls sharply, say by 50%.

The yield levels of NVDY is also higher at around 55% on a 1 year cost basis, compared to MARA. A lot of the payouts have evidently been funded by Nvidia's stellar rally from the April lows. That plus the option layer's performance make a higher expense ratio of 1.27% (compared to MARO's 0.99%) easier to sign up for.

data.title: Delta Offers Rare Value And Resilience In Airline Bankruptcy Wave
data.description:
Delta Air Lines (NYSE:DAL) is set to report its Q3 2025 earnings on October 9. In this article, I provide an earnings preview, examine how analyst estimates have shifted, and update my price target. Since my previous report, DAL stock has surged 27%, outperforming the S&P 500, yet it still trades below my target price reinforcing my buy rating and highlighting further upside potential ahead of the results.

What Earnings Wall Street Analysts Expect For Delta Air Lines

For the third quarter, Wall Street expects revenues of $15.94 billion, indicating 1.7% growth in revenues. The low estimate is $14.89 billion and the high end estimate is $16.29 billion. The revision trends have been positive with a 2.5% upward revision to the estimate over the past six months and less than a percent up over the past one and three months. So, there is no major revision to the estimates over the past few weeks.

Earnings per share are expected to be $1.53 with a $1.40-$1.78 range. This indicates 2% year-on-year growth. We observed that the revision trend has been stronger for earnings per share with an upward revision of nearly 10% over the past six months, 3% up over the past three months and up half a percent over the past month.

A Look At Delta Air Lines’ Guidance

Before the Morgan Stanley 13th Annual Laguna Conference, Delta Air Lines updated its guidance slightly lifting the lower end of its guidance range for revenues. Previously, the airline had expected revenues to be flat or at most four percent up. The most recent guidance lifts the lower end of the growth figure to two percent. Operating margins are expected to be between 9 and 11 percent with earnings per share of $1.25-$1.75. So, the company is expecting revenues a bit ahead of what Wall Street is expecting, but I do believe that this is partially driven by some analysts compiling their revenue estimates on an adjusted basis while others don’t. On earnings per share, we note that the analysts’ mid-point estimate is within the guided range, but Delta’s guidance does contain some downside.

We do note that the company has not missed a single time on revenues in the past 12 quarters. For earnings per share, the company has missed estimates 4 out of 12 times with the last miss produced in Q3 2024.

The Air Travel Market Dynamics Amid A Wave Of Airline Bankruptcies

There is no denying that the market environment is a rough one. Unit revenues had to come back naturally from their post-pandemic peak and that was further accelerated by inflation and macroeconomic uncertainty, most prominently due to trade tensions. Spirit Airlines filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy yet again and also in Europe, we have seen smaller players such as Braathens and Play ceasing operations. So, whatever profit Delta ends up reporting happens amid major industry challenges.

Delta, however, is quite positive about the market trends in general. Corporate travel, which was one part of travel Delta actually expected to be depressed for a long time during the pandemic, is performing very strongly and the same holds for high-yield leisure travel. On the domestic market, there is an inflection as the industry is rationalizing capacity. Transatlantic business is expected to be weak in the third quarter with weak main cabin performance, but strong premium cabin sales. It shows that Delta’s segmentation strategy on which it embarked years ago is paying off. An interesting note during the conference was that the company sees August less as a peak month and October is emerging more as a peak month. If that is a trend that is there to stay, we should see some of the “shoulder” from the peak summer months shift into the fourth quarter. I believe that Delta Air Lines remains positioned well relative to its peers driven by their prudent deployment of capacity, brand value and cabin segmentation strategy.

Delta Air Lines Strength Is Not Reflected In The Stock Price

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

We note that Delta Air Lines is currently trading at 7.1x EV/EBTIDA, which is below the company’s 8x median and below the peer group which trades at 8.5x EV/EBITDA. I don’t believe that this is justified given that the company does various things at the same time that other airlines cannot:

  • The company has increased its dividend by 25%.
  • There is a $1 billion share repurchase program in place.
  • The company is reducing debt.
  • The company can do all of the three things above while its cash pile is still expected to grow.

Optimistically, the price target for Delta Air Lines would be $81.16 representing 45% upside from current prices. Valued against the current EV/EBITDA the price target would be $68.20 indicating 21.5% upside. So, even if the multiple gap doesn’t close we see that Delta Air Lines is still trading at a discount.

Conclusion: Delta Air Lines Remains Underappreciated

I believe that Delta Air Lines remains underappreciated. The current market is a challenging one. We see many airlines struggling, not just in the US but also in Europe. However, Delta Air Lines is seeing some positive inflection in demand as the industry is rationalizing capacity with main cabin being the current weakness as well as Transatlantic travel. We do keep in mind that the bright spots for air travel from macro perspective are brittle. The positives, however, are oil prices that remain depressed and a successful cabin segmentation strategy. So, Delta Air Lines is seeing some pressure on its stock price since September, but it is executing the business better than many of its peers while trading at a discount to those very same peers. I believe that does create an interesting buy opportunity.

data.title: Total Return Forecasts: Major Asset Classes - October 2, 2025
data.description:
The Global Market Index (GMI) is expected to earn a 7%-plus annualized total return in today’s update for the long-run outlook, based on data through September. The estimate is unchanged from the previous month’s analysis.

GMI is a market value-weighted mix of the major asset classes (excluding cash) via ETF proxies. The return forecast is based on the average via three models (defined below). The current 7.1% annualized estimate remains well below the trailing 10-year return for GMI, a market-value weighted mix of the major asset classes (excluding cash).

Most of GMI’s components are expected to generate returns above the pace for trailing 10-year results, with five exceptions: US equities, foreign stocks in developed markets, commodities, US high-yield bonds, and GMI. For example, GMI’s projected 7.1% annualized total return is forecast to deliver a materially softer performance vs. its trailing 9.5% gain for the past decade.

It’s likely that some, most, or possibly all of the forecasts above will be wide of the mark in some degree. GMI’s projections, however, are expected to be somewhat more reliable vs. the estimates for the components. Predictions for the specific markets (US stocks, commodities, etc.) are subject to greater volatility and tracking error compared with aggregating the forecasts into the GMI estimate, a process that may reduce some of the errors through time.

Another way to view the projections above is to use the estimates as a baseline for refining expectations. For instance, the point forecasts above can be adjusted with additional modeling that accounts for other factors not used here. Customizing portfolios for a specific investor to reflect risk tolerance, time horizon, and so on, is also recommended.

For perspective on how GMI’s realized total return has evolved through time, consider the benchmark’s track record on a rolling 10-year annualized basis. The chart below compares GMI’s performance vs. the equivalent for US stocks and US bonds through last month. GMI’s current return for the past ten years is 9.5%, a robust performance that exceeds the previous peak.

Here’s a brief summary of how the forecasts are generated and definitions of the other metrics in the table above:

BB: The Building Block model uses historical returns as a proxy for estimating the future. The sample period used starts in January 1998 (the earliest available date for all the asset classes listed above). The procedure is to calculate the risk premium for each asset class, compute the annualized return and then add an expected risk-free rate to generate a total return forecast. For the expected risk-free rate, we’re using the latest yield on the 10-year Treasury Inflation Protected Security (TIPS). This yield is considered a market estimate of a risk-free, real (inflation-adjusted) return for a “safe” asset — this “risk-free” rate is also used for all the models outlined below. Note that the BB model used here is (loosely) based on a methodology originally outlined by Ibbotson Associates (a division of Morningstar).

EQ: The Equilibrium model reverse engineers expected return by way of risk. Rather than trying to predict return directly, this model relies on the somewhat more reliable framework of using risk metrics to estimate future performance. The process is relatively robust in the sense that forecasting risk is slightly easier than projecting return. The three inputs:

* An estimate of the overall portfolio’s expected market price of risk, defined as the Sharpe ratio, which is the ratio of risk premia to volatility (standard deviation). Note: the “portfolio” here and throughout is defined as GMI

* The expected volatility (standard deviation) of each asset (GMI’s market components)

* The expected correlation for each asset relative to the portfolio (GMI)

This model for estimating equilibrium returns was initially outlined in a 1974 paper by Professor Bill Sharpe. For a summary, see Gary Brinson’s explanation in Chapter 3 of The Portable MBA in Investment. I also review the model in my book Dynamic Asset Allocation. Note that this methodology initially estimates a risk premium and then adds an expected risk-free rate to arrive at total return forecasts. The expected risk-free rate is outlined in BB above.

ADJ: This methodology is identical to the Equilibrium model (EQ) outlined above with one exception: the forecasts are adjusted based on short-term momentum and longer-term mean reversion factors. Momentum is defined as the current price relative to the trailing 12-month moving average. The mean reversion factor is estimated as the current price relative to the trailing 60-month (5-year) moving average. The equilibrium forecasts are adjusted based on current prices relative to the 12-month and 60-month moving averages. If current prices are above (below) the moving averages, the unadjusted risk premia estimates are decreased (increased). The formula for adjustment is simply taking the inverse of the average of the current price to the two moving averages. For example: if an asset class’s current price is 10% above its 12-month moving average and 20% over its 60-month moving average, the unadjusted forecast is reduced by 15% (the average of 10% and 20%). The logic here is that when prices are relatively high vs. recent history, the equilibrium forecasts are reduced. On the flip side, when prices are relatively low vs. recent history, the equilibrium forecasts are increased.

Avg: This column is a simple average of the three forecasts for each row (asset class).

10yr Ret: For perspective on actual returns, this column shows the trailing 10-year annualized total return for the asset classes through the current target month.

Spread: Average-model forecast less trailing 10-year return.
